description: Scaffold the docs-v2 project by copying the RetroUI open-source codebase from ~/Downloads/RetroUI-main, stripping third-party services, switching to Bun, applying the OpenTabs theme, and establishing the project foundation with correct navigation structure for OpenTabs.
qualityChecks: cd docs-v2 && bun run build && bun run type-check && bun run lint

userStories.title: Copy RetroUI source into docs-v2 and establish Bun project
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the RetroUI open-source codebase copied into a new docs-v2/ directory at the repo root, converted to use Bun instead of pnpm, so that we have a working Next.js project as our foundation.
acceptanceCriteria: docs-v2/ directory exists at repo root with the full RetroUI source structure
package.json uses bun-compatible scripts (dev, build, start, lint)
packageManager field removed or set to bun
shadcn registry:build script removed from package.json
bun install succeeds and bun.lock is generated (pnpm-lock.yaml removed)
All third-party service dependencies removed from package.json: @vercel/analytics, @vercel/speed-insights, @wandry/analytics-sdk, resend, @payloadcms/richtext-lexical, @payloadcms/sdk, shadcn
All third-party service code removed: app/actions/newsletter.ts, lib/payload.ts, analytics scripts from app/layout.tsx, Vercel imports from app/layout.tsx
Blog-related files removed: content/blogs/ directory, app/(marketing)/blogs/ directory, Blog document type from contentlayer.config.ts, Author nested type from contentlayer.config.ts
Showcase files removed: app/(marketing)/showcase/ directory, showcase.json
Themes page removed: app/(marketing)/themes/ directory, config/theme.ts, components/CopyTheme.tsx, all .theme-* CSS classes from global.css
preview/ directory removed (RetroUI component previews not needed)
registry.json removed
The .prettierrc and .eslintrc.json are kept
bun run build succeeds (may need to fix import errors from removed files)
A docs-v2/.gitignore exists covering node_modules, .next, .contentlayer
userStories.notes: Source: ~/Downloads/RetroUI-main. Copy the entire directory contents into docs-v2/. Then strip: (1) Remove pnpm-lock.yaml. (2) Edit package.json: remove packageManager field, remove shadcn/registry:build script, remove dependencies @vercel/analytics, @vercel/speed-insights, @wandry/analytics-sdk, resend, @payloadcms/richtext-lexical, @payloadcms/sdk, shadcn. (3) Delete files: app/actions/, lib/payload.ts, content/blogs/, app/(marketing)/blogs/, app/(marketing)/showcase/, app/(marketing)/themes/, showcase.json, registry.json, config/theme.ts, components/CopyTheme.tsx, preview/. (4) In app/layout.tsx: remove Analytics/SpeedInsights imports and JSX, remove Datafast script tag. (5) In contentlayer.config.ts: remove Blog document type, Author nested type, and Blog from documentTypes array. (6) In global.css: remove all .theme-* classes (theme-purple, theme-lime, theme-red, theme-lavender, theme-orange, theme-green and their .dark variants), keep :root, .dark, .with-radius, and utility classes. (7) Run bun install. (8) Fix any broken imports from removed files until bun run build passes. The .github/ and .vscode/ directories from RetroUI can be excluded. IMPORTANT: The existing docs/ directory must NOT be touched — docs-v2/ is a completely separate directory.

cd docs-v2 && bun run build passes
userStories.notes: Replace docs-v2/content/docs/install/index.mdx (and remove nextjs.mdx and vite.mdx since those are RetroUI framework-specific install guides). Create a single installation guide for OpenTabs. The content must be factually accurate: (1) Prerequisites: Bun v1.0+, Google Chrome, MCP-compatible agent. (2) Clone: git clone https://github.com/AnomalyCo/opentabs.git && cd opentabs. (3) Install: bun install. (4) Build: bun run build. (5) Load extension: chrome://extensions -> Developer mode -> Load unpacked -> select ~/.opentabs/extension/. (6) Start server: bun --hot platform/mcp-server/dist/index.js (default port 9515). (7) Connect agent: MCP Streamable HTTP endpoint at http://localhost:9515/mcp, Bearer token from config.json secret field. (8) Verify: opentabs status, or opentabs doctor for full diagnostics, or curl http://localhost:9515/health. Delete the old nextjs.mdx and vite.mdx files.

userStories.title: Create Architecture concept page
userStories.description: As a docs reader, I want to understand the OpenTabs architecture — how the MCP server, Chrome extension, and plugins work together.
acceptanceCriteria: content/docs/concepts/architecture.mdx exists with title 'Architecture'
Page explains the three core components and their roles
Data flow section explains: AI agent -> MCP Streamable HTTP -> MCP Server -> WebSocket -> Chrome Extension -> adapter injection -> page context -> result flows back
Wire protocol section mentions JSON-RPC 2.0 over WebSocket
Security section mentions: WebSocket auth via shared secret, CORS protection, Bearer token for MCP endpoint, adapter hash verification, output sanitization
Hot reload section explains: bun --hot for server, file watchers for plugins, manual extension reload
Content is factually accurate (port 9515, Streamable HTTP at /mcp, WebSocket at /ws)
lastUpdated field is set
cd docs-v2 && bun run build passes
userStories.notes: Create docs-v2/content/docs/concepts/architecture.mdx. Content based on actual codebase research: (1) Overview: three components — MCP Server (platform/mcp-server), Chrome Extension (platform/browser-extension), Plugin SDK (platform/plugin-sdk). (2) Data flow: AI Agent sends tool call via MCP Streamable HTTP POST to /mcp -> Server validates input against JSON Schema (pre-compiled Ajv) -> Server sends tool.dispatch JSON-RPC over WebSocket to extension -> Extension finds best matching tab by URL pattern + ranking (active tab preferred) -> Extension executes chrome.scripting.executeScript in MAIN world -> Adapter's handle() runs with page's cookies/localStorage/DOM -> Result flows back. (3) Wire protocol: JSON-RPC 2.0 over WebSocket. Key messages: sync.full, plugin.update, tool.dispatch, tool.invocationStart/End, tab.stateChanged, tab.syncAll, ping/pong. (4) Security: WebSocket auth via Sec-WebSocket-Protocol header with shared secret from ~/.opentabs/config.json. CORS rejects browser Origin headers (except chrome-extension://). Bearer token auth for /mcp. Rate limiting on admin endpoints. Adapter hash verification + Object.freeze. Prompt injection detection in tool descriptions. Output sanitization (__proto__, constructor, prototype key removal). (5) Hot reload: bun --hot for server (HotState pattern on globalThis), file watchers for plugin manifest + IIFE changes, extension requires manual reload from chrome://extensions.

userStories.title: Create Plugin System concept page
userStories.description: As a docs reader, I want to understand how the OpenTabs plugin system works — discovery, registration, tool prefixing, and the adapter injection model.
acceptanceCriteria: content/docs/concepts/plugin-system.mdx exists with title 'Plugin System'
Plugin discovery section explains two sources: local paths from ~/.opentabs/config.json plugins array, npm packages matching opentabs-plugin-* or with opentabs-plugin keyword
Tool prefixing section explains that tools are exposed as <plugin>_<tool> (e.g., slack_send_message)
Adapter injection section explains: IIFE bundles injected via chrome.scripting.executeScript into MAIN world, registers on globalThis.__openTabs.adapters
Plugin manifest section explains opentabs-plugin.json format
Trust tiers mentioned: official (@opentabs-dev/ scope), community, local
Content is factually accurate
lastUpdated field is set
cd docs-v2 && bun run build passes
userStories.notes: Create docs-v2/content/docs/concepts/plugin-system.mdx. Content: (1) Overview: plugins are standalone npm packages extending OpenTabsPlugin. (2) Discovery: MCP server reads ~/.opentabs/config.json plugins array for local paths + scans node_modules for opentabs-plugin-* packages. Local paths must be under $HOME or $TMPDIR (security). npm plugins must be in npmPlugins allow-list. (3) Plugin manifest (opentabs-plugin.json): contains name, version, displayName, description, url_patterns (Chrome match patterns), tools array with name, displayName, description, icon, input_schema, output_schema (JSON Schema from Zod), adapterHash. (4) Tool prefixing: tools exposed to MCP as <plugin>_<tool> (e.g., slack_send_message). Prevents name collisions. (5) Adapter injection: opentabs build bundles plugin into dist/adapter.iife.js. Server writes IIFE to ~/.opentabs/extension/adapters/<plugin>.js. Extension injects via chrome.scripting.executeScript({files: [...], world: 'MAIN'}). File-based injection bypasses CSP. Adapter registers at globalThis.__openTabs.adapters[pluginName]. (6) Trust tiers: official (@opentabs-dev/), community, local — tier prefixed to MCP tool descriptions. (7) File watcher: server watches local plugin dirs for manifest + IIFE changes, auto-syncs to extension via plugin.update.

userStories.title: Create Tab State Machine concept page
userStories.description: As a docs reader, I want to understand the tab state machine — the three states (closed, unavailable, ready) and how they affect tool dispatch.
acceptanceCriteria: content/docs/concepts/tab-state-machine.mdx exists with title 'Tab State Machine'
Three states explained: closed (no matching tab), unavailable (tab exists but isReady() returns false), ready (tab exists and isReady() returns true)
State computation explained: extension queries tabs by URL pattern, probes adapter readiness via isReady()
Tab ranking explained: active tab in focused window (rank 3) > active tab in any window (rank 2) > any tab in focused window (rank 1) > other tabs (rank 0)
State change notifications mentioned: tab.stateChanged, tab.syncAll
Content is factually accurate
lastUpdated field is set
cd docs-v2 && bun run build passes
userStories.notes: Create docs-v2/content/docs/concepts/tab-state-machine.mdx. Content based on actual tab-state.ts and tab-matching.ts in platform/browser-extension/src/. (1) Overview: each plugin has three tab states, computed per-plugin. (2) States: closed — no Chrome tab matches the plugin's url_patterns; unavailable — matching tab exists but adapter's isReady() returns false (e.g., user not logged in); ready — matching tab with isReady() returning true. (3) Computation: extension uses chrome.tabs.query({url: pattern}) to find matching tabs, then probes each via chrome.scripting.executeScript calling adapter.isReady(). If ANY matching tab is ready, plugin state is ready. (4) Tab ranking: when multiple tabs match, they're ranked for tool dispatch: active tab in focused window (3), active tab in any window (2), any tab in focused window (1), other (0). Tool dispatch tries tabs in rank order with fallback. (5) Notifications: extension reports state changes to server via tab.stateChanged. Full sync via tab.syncAll on WebSocket connect/reconnect.

Network: browser_enable_network_capture, browser_get_network_requests, browser_disable_network_capture. Console: browser_get_console_logs, browser_clear_console_logs. Extension: browser_reload_extension, browser_extension_get_state, browser_extension_get_logs, browser_extension_get_side_panel, browser_extension_check_adapter, browser_extension_force_reconnect. Present as a table with tool name, category, and brief description.

userStories.title: Create Plugin SDK docs page
userStories.description: As a plugin developer, I want comprehensive documentation on the Plugin SDK — the OpenTabsPlugin base class, defineTool factory, and Zod schema requirements.
acceptanceCriteria: content/docs/plugins/plugin-sdk.mdx exists with title 'Plugin SDK'
OpenTabsPlugin base class documented with all abstract properties: name, version, description, displayName, urlPatterns, tools, isReady(), teardown()
defineTool factory documented with all config properties: name (snake_case), displayName, description, icon (Lucide icon name), input (Zod schema), output (Zod schema), handle function
Zod schema requirements documented: no .transform() (JSON Schema serialization), no .pipe()/.preprocess(), .refine() callbacks must not throw
handle function context explained: runs in browser MAIN world, has access to DOM, cookies, localStorage, window globals
ToolError class documented for structured error reporting
Content is factually accurate based on actual SDK source
lastUpdated field is set
cd docs-v2 && bun run build passes
userStories.notes: Create docs-v2/content/docs/plugins/plugin-sdk.mdx. Content based on platform/plugin-sdk/src/index.ts. (1) OpenTabsPlugin abstract class: name (string, lowercase alphanumeric + hyphens), version (semver), description, displayName, urlPatterns (Chrome match patterns like *://*.slack.com/*), tools (ToolDefinition[]), isReady() (async, returns boolean — readiness probe, e.g., check if user is logged in), teardown() (optional cleanup before re-injection). (2) defineTool<TInput, TOutput>(config): name (snake_case), displayName, description (shown to AI agents — must be accurate), icon (kebab-case Lucide icon name from LUCIDE_ICON_NAMES set), input (Zod schema for parameters), output (Zod schema for return value), handle(params) (async function that runs in browser page MAIN world context). (3) Zod constraints: schemas serialized to JSON Schema via z.toJSONSchema() for MCP protocol. Never use .transform() (not representable in JSON Schema). Avoid .pipe(), .preprocess(), effects. .refine() callbacks must catch errors (Zod 4 runs refine even on failed base validation). (4) ToolError: import {ToolError} from '@opentabs-dev/plugin-sdk'. Has machine-readable code field. (5) handle() context: executes in the page's MAIN world via chrome.scripting.executeScript. Has full access to page DOM, window, cookies, localStorage, fetch (same-origin with page's session).

userStories.title: Create 'Creating a Plugin' guide
userStories.description: As a plugin developer, I want a step-by-step guide to creating, building, and registering an OpenTabs plugin.
acceptanceCriteria: content/docs/plugins/creating-a-plugin.mdx exists with title 'Creating a Plugin'
Step 1: Scaffold with create-opentabs-plugin CLI (bunx create-opentabs-plugin my-plugin --domain .example.com --display 'My App')
Step 2: Plugin structure explained (src/index.ts, src/tools/, package.json, tsconfig.json)
Step 3: Define tools with defineTool() — example with input/output Zod schemas
Step 4: Implement isReady() — explain how to check authentication/page state
Step 5: Build with opentabs build (or bun run build which runs tsc + opentabs build)
Step 6: Register — explain that create-opentabs-plugin auto-registers in ~/.opentabs/config.json, or manually add path
Step 7: Test — start server, open target app in Chrome, use AI agent to invoke tools
opentabs build --watch mode mentioned for development
Content is factually accurate
lastUpdated field is set
cd docs-v2 && bun run build passes
userStories.notes: Create docs-v2/content/docs/plugins/creating-a-plugin.mdx. Based on actual create-plugin source (platform/create-plugin/src/index.ts) and cli (platform/cli/src/commands/build.ts). Steps: (1) Scaffold: bunx create-opentabs-plugin my-plugin --domain .example.com --display 'My App'. Also available via: opentabs plugin create my-plugin --domain .example.com. Scaffolds: package.json (with @opentabs-dev/plugin-sdk, @opentabs-dev/cli deps), tsconfig.json, eslint.config.ts, .prettierrc, .gitignore, src/index.ts (extends OpenTabsPlugin), src/tools/example.ts (defineTool example). Auto-registers in ~/.opentabs/config.json. (2) Plugin structure: src/index.ts — plugin class, src/tools/ — one file per tool. (3) Define a tool: show defineTool with name, displayName, description, icon, input (z.object), output (z.object), handle function. (4) Implement isReady: example checking for auth token in localStorage or specific DOM element. (5) Build: cd my-plugin && bun run build (runs tsc then opentabs build). Generates opentabs-plugin.json manifest + dist/adapter.iife.js. Watch mode: opentabs build --watch. (6) Register: create-opentabs-plugin auto-registers. Manual: opentabs plugin add ./path/to/plugin, or add path to ~/.opentabs/config.json plugins array. (7) Test: start server (opentabs dev), open target app, ask AI agent to use the tool.

userStories.title: Create Slack plugin docs page
userStories.description: As a docs reader, I want to see what the Slack plugin can do — all 21 tools with descriptions — as a real-world example of an OpenTabs plugin.
acceptanceCriteria: content/docs/plugins/slack.mdx exists with title 'Slack Plugin'
Page shows plugin metadata: name 'slack', URL pattern *://*.slack.com/*, 21 tools

All 21 tools listed with accurate names and descriptions: send_message, edit_message, delete_message, read_messages, read_thread, search_messages, list_channels, get_channel_info, create_channel, set_channel_topic, set_channel_purpose, invite_to_channel, list_members, get_user_profile, list_users, open_dm, upload_file, list_files, add_reaction, remove_reaction, pin_message, unpin_message
Tools organized by category: Messages, Channels, Users, Files, Reactions, Pins
Authentication section explains it uses the user's existing Slack session (no API tokens needed)
Usage examples showing MCP tool names with prefix (slack_send_message, slack_read_messages, etc.)
Content is factually accurate based on actual plugin source
lastUpdated field is set
cd docs-v2 && bun run build passes
userStories.notes: Create docs-v2/content/docs/plugins/slack.mdx. Based on actual plugins/slack/ source. (1) Overview: the Slack plugin gives AI agents full access to Slack through the user's authenticated browser session. No Slack API tokens or OAuth needed. (2) Metadata: name: slack, URL pattern: *://*.slack.com/*, version from plugin source. (3) Authentication: extracts session tokens automatically from the Slack web app (localConfig, boot_data, Redux store, etc.). The user just needs to be logged into Slack in Chrome. (4) Tools by category — Messages: slack_send_message (send to channel or thread), slack_edit_message (edit own messages), slack_delete_message, slack_read_messages (with date filtering + pagination), slack_read_thread (with pagination), slack_search_messages (across channels with sorting). Channels: slack_list_channels (with filters), slack_get_channel_info (topic, purpose, members), slack_create_channel (public/private), slack_set_channel_topic, slack_set_channel_purpose, slack_invite_to_channel. Users: slack_list_members (channel member IDs), slack_get_user_profile (full profile), slack_list_users (workspace users), slack_open_dm (1:1 or group). Files: slack_upload_file (text or base64 binary), slack_list_files (with filters). Reactions: slack_add_reaction, slack_remove_reaction. Pins: slack_pin_message, slack_unpin_message. Present as tables with tool name, description. Note: total is 22 tools (send, edit, delete, read_messages, read_thread, search, list_channels, get_channel_info, create_channel, set_topic, set_purpose, invite, list_members, get_user_profile, list_users, open_dm, upload_file, list_files, add_reaction, remove_reaction, pin, unpin).

userStories.title: Create Configuration reference page
userStories.description: As a user, I want to understand the ~/.opentabs/config.json configuration file format and all available options.
acceptanceCriteria: content/docs/reference/configuration.mdx exists with title 'Configuration'
Config file location explained: ~/.opentabs/config.json (overridable via OPENTABS_CONFIG_DIR env)
All config fields documented: plugins (array of local paths), tools (object of tool_name: boolean for enable/disable), secret (64-char hex, auto-generated), npmPlugins (array of npm package names)
Example config.json shown with realistic values

Content is factually accurate
lastUpdated field is set
cd docs-v2 && bun run build passes
userStories.notes: Create docs-v2/content/docs/reference/configuration.mdx. Based on platform/mcp-server/src/config.ts. (1) Location: ~/.opentabs/config.json, auto-created on first server run. Override with OPENTABS_CONFIG_DIR env var. (2) Fields: plugins (string[]) — local plugin paths, relative to config dir or absolute, must be under $HOME or $TMPDIR; tools (Record<string, boolean>) — per-tool enable/disable overrides, e.g., {"slack_send_message": false} disables that tool; secret (string) — 64-char hex WebSocket auth secret, auto-generated, used for Bearer token auth on /mcp and WebSocket Sec-WebSocket-Protocol; npmPlugins (string[]) — npm package names allowed for discovery, e.g., ["opentabs-plugin-foo"]. (3) Example: {"plugins": ["../plugins/slack"], "tools": {}, "secret": "a1b2c3...", "npmPlugins": []}. (4) Env vars: PORT (default 9515), OPENTABS_CONFIG_DIR.

userStories.title: Create CLI Commands reference page

All CLI commands documented: opentabs dev, opentabs build, opentabs build --watch, opentabs status, opentabs doctor, opentabs setup, opentabs plugin create, opentabs plugin list, opentabs plugin add, opentabs plugin remove, opentabs config, opentabs logs, opentabs reload
Each command has usage syntax, description, and options where applicable
Content is factually accurate
lastUpdated field is set
cd docs-v2 && bun run build passes
userStories.notes: Create docs-v2/content/docs/reference/cli.mdx. Based on platform/cli/src/ source. Commands: (1) opentabs dev — start MCP server with hot reload (bun --hot). Options: --port (default 9515). (2) opentabs build — build current plugin (manifest + IIFE adapter). Must be run from plugin directory. Options: --watch (rebuild on tsc output changes). (3) opentabs status — show server status, connected plugins, tool counts. (4) opentabs doctor — full diagnostic: checks Bun version, config file, server reachability, extension connection, plugin status. (5) opentabs setup — install extension files to ~/.opentabs/extension/. (6) opentabs plugin create <name> — scaffold new plugin. Options: --domain (URL domain), --display (display name). (7) opentabs plugin list — list configured plugins. (8) opentabs plugin add <path> — add plugin path to config.json. (9) opentabs plugin remove <name> — remove plugin from config.json. (10) opentabs config — manage config. (11) opentabs logs — view server logs. (12) opentabs reload — trigger server config/plugin rediscovery.

userStories.title: Create MCP Server reference page
userStories.description: As a developer integrating with OpenTabs, I want to understand the MCP server's HTTP endpoints and WebSocket protocol.
acceptanceCriteria: content/docs/reference/mcp-server.mdx exists with title 'MCP Server'
All HTTP endpoints documented: /mcp (POST/GET/DELETE — MCP Streamable HTTP), /ws (WebSocket), /ws-info (GET), /health (GET), /reload (POST), /extension/reload (POST)
Authentication explained: Bearer token for /mcp, WebSocket auth via Sec-WebSocket-Protocol header
MCP client configuration example for Claude Code / Cursor
Content is factually accurate (port 9515, correct endpoint paths)
lastUpdated field is set
cd docs-v2 && bun run build passes
userStories.notes: Create docs-v2/content/docs/reference/mcp-server.mdx. Based on platform/mcp-server/src/http-routes.ts and index.ts. (1) Endpoints table: /mcp POST/GET/DELETE — MCP Streamable HTTP transport (AI clients connect here); /ws WebSocket — Chrome extension connection; /ws-info GET — returns WebSocket URL + auth secret; /health GET — server status (plugins, tools, uptime, reloadCount); /reload POST — trigger config/plugin rediscovery; /extension/reload POST — signal extension to reload. (2) Auth: /mcp requires Bearer token (from config.json secret). /reload and /extension/reload also require Bearer token + rate limited (10 req/min). /ws uses Sec-WebSocket-Protocol header. (3) CORS: rejects requests with browser Origin headers (except chrome-extension://). (4) MCP client config example for Claude Code: add to ~/.claude.json or project .mcp.json: {"mcpServers": {"opentabs": {"type": "streamable-http", "url": "http://localhost:9515/mcp", "headers": {"Authorization": "Bearer <secret>"}}}}. (5) Default port: 9515 (configurable via PORT env).

userStories.title: Create Troubleshooting reference page
userStories.description: As a user encountering issues, I want a troubleshooting guide covering common problems and solutions.
acceptanceCriteria: content/docs/reference/troubleshooting.mdx exists with title 'Troubleshooting'

Covers: extension not connecting (check server running, correct port, extension loaded), plugin showing as unavailable (check isReady, user logged in), tool dispatch timeout (30s default), hot reload not working (bun --hot file watcher stale after 22+ hours), extension changes not taking effect (manual reload required)
opentabs doctor command mentioned as first diagnostic step
Content is factually accurate
lastUpdated field is set
cd docs-v2 && bun run build passes
userStories.notes: Create docs-v2/content/docs/reference/troubleshooting.mdx. Common issues: (1) Extension not connecting: check server is running (curl http://localhost:9515/health), check extension loaded in chrome://extensions, check extension side panel shows connection status, run opentabs doctor for full diagnostic. (2) Plugin showing as 'unavailable': the tab matches URL patterns but isReady() returns false — usually means user isn't logged in. Solution: log into the web app in Chrome. (3) Plugin showing as 'closed': no tab matches URL patterns. Solution: open the target web app. (4) Tool dispatch timeout: default 30s timeout. If the tool's handle() function takes too long, it times out. (5) Hot reload not working: bun --hot file watchers can go stale on long-running processes (22+ hours). Solution: restart the MCP server process. Check via /health endpoint reloadCount. (6) Extension changes not taking effect: Chrome extension does NOT auto-reload. After bun run build, must manually reload from chrome://extensions (click refresh icon). Close and reopen side panel. (7) Plugin changes not picked up: file watcher should auto-detect. If not, trigger manually: opentabs reload or POST /reload. (8) First step: always run opentabs doctor — it checks Bun version, config, server, extension, plugins comprehensively.

userStories.notes: The RetroUI source already has .eslintrc.json and .prettierrc. Ensure: (1) tsconfig.json has strict: true (it already does). (2) package.json scripts include: type-check: 'tsc --noEmit', lint: 'next lint' (or eslint .), lint:fix: 'next lint --fix', format: 'prettier --write .', format:check: 'prettier --check .'. The RetroUI source's .eslintrc.json extends 'next/core-web-vitals' which should be fine. (3) Verify all three commands pass: bun run build, bun run type-check, bun run lint. Fix any type errors or lint issues from the content changes made in previous stories.

userStories.notes: Final verification story. Run all three checks: bun run build, bun run type-check, bun run lint. If any fail, fix the issues. Common things to check: (1) Broken imports from removed files. (2) Contentlayer document type changes (Blog type removed). (3) MDX pages referencing removed components (ComponentShowcase, ComponentSource). (4) Navigation links pointing to removed pages. (5) Image references to removed files. (6) ThemeWrapper/ThemeContext references to removed theme variants. After fixing, verify the dev server renders correctly at localhost:3000 (or whatever port Next.js uses).
